planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; Chinese redbud is notably more shade-tolerant than Eastern redbud and adapts well to understory positions. The cultivar 'Avondale' has the most intensely colored, nearly purple-rose flowers and is one of the most spectacular spring-flowering woody plants available in small sizes.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic soil in full sun to part shade; Chinese redbud is a multi-stemmed shrub or small tree from central China that produces an extraordinary spring flowering display on bare branches in early spring — densely packed, vibrant rosy-purple pea flowers covering every branch, twig, and even directly on older wood (cauliflory) before a single leaf emerges. The intensity of the floral display is arguably greater than Eastern redbud (Cercis canadensis) because the flowers are deeper in color (deeper rose to violet-magenta) and the shrubby habit places them at eye level where the bloom can be most appreciated. The cultivar 'Avondale' (from New Zealand) is considered among the finest woody ornamentals in cultivation, with unusually intense, deep rose-purple flowers. Chinese redbud is more shade-tolerant and more compact than Eastern redbud, making it more manageable in smaller gardens and better suited to underplanting in partial shade. The heart-shaped leaves are large and glossy, turning yellow in fall. Long-lived, disease-resistant, and exceptionally showy in spring.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in moist, humus-rich, well-drained, acidic soil in full to part shade; Japanese anise is a slow-growing evergreen that thrives in conditions where many broad-leaved evergreens fail — deep shade, dry shade under trees, and difficult urban positions. It is completely heat-tolerant and is more adaptable to humid Gulf Coast conditions than its native range in Japan might suggest.

directions: Plant in moist to moderately dry, humus-rich, acidic soil in full to part shade; Japanese anise is a slow-growing, densely leafy evergreen shrub native to Japan and Taiwan, closely related to Florida anise (Illicium floridanum) but with wider cold hardiness and glossier foliage with a strong, pleasant anise-clove fragrance when crushed. In spring it produces small, cream-white to pale-yellow star-shaped flowers with many narrow petals — understated but charming close up, with the same star pattern as culinary star anise (I. verum) though unrelated in flavor. The foliage is the primary landscape asset: thick, lustrous, deep-green leaves densely arranged on upright stems creating a solid, refined mound of evergreen texture in shade gardens where few alternatives exist. It is outstanding as a shade-garden shrub under large trees, as an evergreen screen in shade, and in combination with hostas, hellebores, and woodland ferns. CAUTION: all parts of the plant are toxic if ingested — the seeds resemble and may be confused with culinary star anise.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in consistently moist, acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil in part shade; fever tree grows naturally at the edges of swamps, pocosin bogs, and moist woodlands in the southeastern coastal plain. It requires consistent soil moisture and performs poorly in dry soils or full sun, making it an ideal plant for moist shade or rain garden settings.

directions: Plant in consistently moist, acidic, humus-rich soil in part shade; fever tree is one of the rarest and most unusual native shrubs of the southeastern coastal plain, found naturally only in a narrow band of swampy pocosins and wet pine savannas in coastal Georgia and the Carolinas. In the coffee family (Rubiaceae), it is related to gardenias and quinine trees — and indeed the bark was historically used as a quinine substitute to treat malaria (hence 'fever tree'). In early summer, the plant produces clusters of small, tubular, rose-to-magenta flowers, each surrounded by spectacular, greatly enlarged, rose-pink to rosy-red sepals (calyx lobes) that resemble large pink bracts or petals and create a showy, ornamental display far larger than the flowers themselves — similar in strategy to dogwood bracts. The large, tropical-looking leaves give the shrub a bold texture unusual among native plants. A conservation-significant plant that also makes an outstanding specimen for moist shaded gardens in zones 7–9, appreciating conditions similar to native azaleas.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in fall in very well-drained, lean, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; flannel bush is extremely drought-tolerant and native to dry slopes and chaparral — it is killed by summer irrigation or waterlogged soils. Establish without supplemental watering beyond what is needed for the first one to two summers and then cease irrigation entirely. All plant parts cause skin, eye, and respiratory irritation from tiny barbed hairs.

directions: Plant in sharply drained, lean, dry soil in full sun with NO summer irrigation once established; flannel bush is one of California's most spectacular native shrubs, covering itself in large (1.5–2.5 inch), saucer-shaped, intensely golden-yellow flowers from April through June — on a mature specimen the display of hundreds of golden flowers against the dark, lobed, flannel-textured, gray-green leaves is one of the showiest spectacles in the native California garden. The flowers technically lack petals; the bright-yellow 'petals' are enlarged, petal-like sepals. Named for the distinctive, flannel-soft texture of the leaves and stems, which are densely covered with stellate hairs. The plant strongly prefers lean, well-drained, dry soils and dry summers — conditions that suit California native planting schemes and water-wise gardens, but make it difficult in climates with summer rainfall. The hybrid 'Ken Taylor' (F. californicum x F. mexicanum) is the most commonly cultivated, offering extra-large flowers over an extended season. CAUTION: the fine hairs are a serious skin and eye irritant — wear gloves and eye protection when pruning.

planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container-grown trees in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in spring or fall; apple serviceberry is extremely adaptable to a wide range of soils and moisture levels and is one of the most reliable multi-season small trees for temperate gardens. 'Autumn Brilliance' is the most popular cultivar for its consistent orange-red fall color and larger, sweeter fruit.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral, adaptable soil in full sun to part shade; apple serviceberry is the most widely planted Amelanchier hybrid — a cross between A. arborea and A. laevis producing larger flowers and larger, juicier berries than either parent. In early to mid-spring, before or just as the leaves emerge, it produces a spectacular display of pure-white flowers in drooping clusters that are among the first flowers of the season for early native bees, mining bees, and other spring pollinators that emerge before many plants are flowering. The blueberry-like fruits that ripen in June are sweet, purple-black, and edible fresh, in pies, jams, and wine — they are exceptionally rich in antioxidants and have a pleasant flavor combining blueberry and mild almond notes. The fruits are intensely attractive to birds. In fall, the foliage turns brilliant shades of orange, red, and occasionally yellow, providing a final season of interest. Multi-season value: spring flowers, summer edible fruit, fall color, ornamental winter silhouette. Tolerates part shade and is among the most wildlife-valuable small trees available.
